summary: Gruber details Apple's new App Store review features, which limit how often developers can prompt users for reviews to three times per year, add a system-wide opt-out switch, and allow developers to reply to reviews for the first time. He explains the technical details of how the new system works, including that the prompt counter doesn't reset with app updates. He also identifies the root cause of review-begging: Apple resets an app's average rating with each update, incentivizing developers to repeatedly ask for new reviews. Gruber sees these changes as meaningful progress but notes Apple still needs to fix the rating-reset policy to fully solve the problem.
key_points: Developer review replies will be visually connected to user reviews, with each side limited to one response that can be edited — a feature developers have wanted since 2008
The three-prompts-per-year limit doesn't reset with app updates, and users who have already rated or dismissed three prompts won't be asked again for a year
Apple's policy of resetting average ratings with each app update is the root cause of aggressive review prompting, and Apple acknowledges this but has no fix to announce
A system-wide master switch will let users disable all review prompts from all apps entirely
The new API will eventually become the only sanctioned way to prompt for reviews, though Apple has no enforcement timeline yet
money_quotes: Developers have been clamoring for something like this ever since the App Store opened in 2008.
An individual app can prompt three times for a review per year, period. This counter does not get reset each time the developer updates their app. Good.
From a developer's perspective, it sucks when you replace a highly-rated version of your app with a minor bug-fix update and your average rating gets erased.
It's a tricky problem to solve, though — sometimes the latest update of an app really does deserve a new average rating, for better or for worse.

I've long considered a public campaign against this particular practice, wherein I'd encourage Daring Fireball readers, whenever they encounter these 'Please rate this app' prompts, to go ahead and take the time to do it — but to rate the app with just one star.
A limit of three prompts per year, and a system-wide switch to turn off all such prompts, go a long way toward fixing the problem from the user's perspective.
If Apple can figure out a fairer way to compute the average rating for apps across updates, they can help solve it from a developer's standpoint too.
key_insight: Apple's new review prompt limits address the symptom of apps badgering users, but the root cause — resetting average ratings with each app update — remains an unsolved problem that incentivizes the behavior in the first place.
